content.text: What TCO Means and Why It Matters

Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) is the complete financial cost of an asset or system over its entire useful life — not just the purchase price. For IT infrastructure, TCO extends far beyond hardware invoices to include facilities, staffing, licences, maintenance, and the cost of downtime.

When comparing on-premises infrastructure to cloud, using only the headline server quote versus AWS pricing produces a misleading result. A rigorous TCO analysis surfaces all costs on both sides over a consistent time horizon — typically three to five years — so the comparison is honest.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side 5-year TCO comparison: on-premises $1.21M vs AWS $326,700 — 73% saving when accounting for all cost categories
attrs.title: Total Cost of Ownership: On-Premises vs AWS

content.text: The Seven Categories of On-Premises TCO
On-premises TCO is commonly underestimated because teams focus on the hardware budget and overlook the supporting cost structure. A complete on-premises cost model includes seven categories:
Cost Category
On-Premises Reality
AWS Equivalent
Server hardware
Large upfront CapEx; 3–5 year refresh cycles; residual value often zero
Included in EC2 hourly rate; AWS absorbs refresh
Networking
Switches, routers, cables, cross-connects; ongoing maintenance contracts
VPC, ENI, and inter-AZ networking included; egress billed separately
Storage
SAN/NAS arrays provisioned for peak; low average utilisation; proprietary licences
Pay per GB used (S3) or provisioned (EBS); unlimited growth with no upfront
Facilities
Data centre floor space, power draw, cooling (typically 1.4–2× PUE), physical security
Included in AWS pricing; AWS runs at PUE close to 1.2
IT staff
System administrators, storage engineers, network engineers — ongoing salaries and benefits
Managed services reduce ops burden; staff shifts to architecture and innovation
Software licences
OS, database, virtualisation, monitoring, backup — each with renewal and support contracts
Linux OS included; Windows BYOL or licence-included pricing; RDS includes DB engine
Disaster recovery
Secondary data centre, replication hardware, DRaaS contracts, regular failover testing cost
Multi-AZ, cross-region replication, Route 53 failover — available on-demand, no second site CapEx
Hidden On-Premises Costs That Inflate TCO
Beyond the seven explicit categories, on-premises infrastructure carries opportunity and risk costs that rarely appear in initial budget requests but dominate long-term TCO.
Over-provisioning for peak load:
On-premises capacity must be sized for the peak demand moment. The average utilisation of enterprise servers is 15–20 %. The cost of the idle 80–85 % is pure waste in a fixed-cost model.
Long procurement cycles:
Ordering, delivering, racking, and configuring new servers takes weeks to months. The business pays for that lead time in delayed launches and missed revenue.
Stranded capacity after downsizing:
Hardware cannot be returned when a project ends or headcount falls. Organisations that acquired capacity for a peak that never materialised cannot recover that CapEx.
Security and compliance overhead:
PCI, HIPAA, and SOC 2 audits require extensive evidence gathering for physical controls. AWS Artifact provides pre-certified compliance reports that eliminate weeks of documentation effort.
Downtime cost:

Hardware failures in single-site deployments mean hours of downtime. Gartner estimates IT downtime costs $5,600 per minute for large enterprises. Cloud Multi-AZ reduces downtime probability by orders of magnitude.
AWS Pricing Calculator — Building a TCO Model

The AWS Pricing Calculator (at calculator.aws) lets you model a proposed architecture and generate a monthly and annual cost estimate. For TCO comparisons, it includes a side-by-side on-premises vs cloud section that accounts for the commonly missed cost categories.
Create a new estimate and add AWS services (EC2, RDS, S3, CloudFront, etc.) matching your on-premises workload.
Select the pricing model for each service: On-Demand for variable workloads, Savings Plans or RIs for stable baseline.
Export the estimate as a CSV or share a link. The export includes line items for each service, region, and pricing tier.
In the on-premises section, enter current hardware costs, rack space rates, power/cooling PUE, staff headcount, and licence renewals.
The calculator generates a five-year TCO comparison, typically showing 40–70 % savings for workloads moving from on-premises to AWS.

The Pricing Calculator estimate reflects published rates without negotiated discounts. Enterprise customers with EDP (Enterprise Discount Programme) agreements receive additional percentage reductions that further improve the cloud TCO.
The Migration Economics View

A TCO comparison for a migration decision must include three time periods: the current state (on-premises run rate), the transition period (parallel running costs during migration), and the future state (cloud-only costs after full migration). Ignoring the transition period understates total migration cost.
Period
On-Premises Cost
AWS Cost
Notes
Pre-migration (Year 0)
Full run rate
Migration tooling, assessments
AWS MAP programme may offset
Transition (Year 1)
Declining (as apps migrate)
Rising (new workloads on AWS)
Dual-run costs — plan for this
Post-migration (Years 2–5)
Zero (facilities decommissioned)
Stable; decreasing with RIs
Full savings materialise here

AWS Migration Acceleration Programme (MAP) provides funding credits and professional services to offset transition-period costs for qualifying enterprise migrations. The credit is applied to your AWS bill as a draw-down over the migration period.
Querying Your Current AWS Spend for TCO Reporting

For existing AWS workloads, Cost Explorer and the Cost and Usage Report (CUR) provide the data needed to calculate actual run-rate TCO and project forward. The following commands extract the cost data most useful in a TCO model.

# 1. Last 12 months actual spend by service (unblended cost)
aws ce get-cost-and-usage \
  --time-period Start=$(date -d '12 months ago' +%Y-%m-01),End=$(date +%Y-%m-01) \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --metrics UnblendedCost \
  --group-by Type=DIMENSION,Key=SERVICE \
  --query 'ResultsByTime[*].{Month:TimePeriod.Start,Groups:Groups[*].{Service:Keys[0],Cost:Metrics.UnblendedCost.Amount}}' \
  --output json > tco-spend-by-service.json

# 2. Estimated savings from Savings Plans / RIs in the same period
aws ce get-savings-plans-utilization \
  --time-period Start=$(date -d '12 months ago' +%Y-%m-01),End=$(date +%Y-%m-01) \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --query 'SavingsPlansUtilizationsByTime[*].{Month:TimePeriod.Start,NetSavings:Total.NetSavings.Amount}' \
  --output table

# 3. Cost forecast for next 12 months
aws ce get-cost-forecast \
  --time-period Start=$(date +%Y-%m-01),End=$(date -d '12 months' +%Y-%m-01) \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --metric UNBLENDED_COST \
  --query 'ForecastResultsByTime[*].{Month:TimePeriod.Start,Cost:MeanValue}' \
  --output table
The AWS TCO Calculator vs the Pricing Calculator
AWS provides two distinct cost-estimation tools that serve different purposes in a TCO analysis. Understanding which to use avoids building a model in the wrong tool.
Tool
Purpose
Best Used When
AWS Pricing Calculator
Estimate AWS service costs for a defined architecture
You already know the target architecture and want an accurate cost figure for it
AWS Migration Evaluator (formerly TSO Logic)
Ingest actual server utilisation data and model right-sized AWS equivalents
You want a bottoms-up TCO estimate from actual inventory, not a theoretical architecture
Cost Explorer
Analyse actual historical spend and forecast forward
You are already on AWS and need run-rate TCO reporting for existing workloads
AWS Cost and Usage Report
Line-item cost data for finance systems, chargebacks, and BI tools
You need granular, queryable cost data to build custom TCO dashboards in Athena or QuickSight
AWS Migration Evaluator Deep Dive

AWS Migration Evaluator (formerly TSO Logic) is the purpose-built TCO assessment tool for pre-migration decisions. It works from actual data rather than estimates, producing a TCO model that holds up to finance scrutiny.
Discovery:
A lightweight agentless collector imports server inventory from VMware vCenter, AWS Application Discovery Service, or manual upload of a spreadsheet.
Rightsizing model:
It analyses 90 days of CPU, RAM, and storage utilisation data to select the smallest AWS instance that meets the P95 demand peak — eliminating on-premises over-provisioning from the comparison.
Cost inputs:
You enter on-premises unit costs (server amortisation rate, rack space cost per U, kWh rate, PUE factor, staff cost per server) so the model uses your actual numbers, not industry averages.
Output:
A directional business case report with a five-year TCO comparison, recommended AWS instance types for each workload, and the estimated savings per application group.
Staff Cost Modelling

Labour is typically the largest single component of on-premises TCO, yet it is the hardest to model honestly because it involves assumptions about role changes rather than elimination. The accurate framing is not 'we will lay off the data centre team' but 'the data centre team's effort will shift from undifferentiated heavy lifting to value-creating work.'
Activities AWS eliminates:
Hardware procurement, rack and stack, firmware patching, physical-layer troubleshooting, cooling system maintenance, and generator testing.
Activities that remain or grow:
Application architecture, cloud cost governance (FinOps), security policy, performance tuning, and CI/CD pipeline management.
FTE savings model:

A common model credits 0.5 FTE per 100 migrated servers (to account for the elimination of physical-layer work) while retaining the team for cloud operations. This is conservative; actual savings vary by automation maturity.
Licence Optimisation in TCO
Software licence costs are a significant line item in on-premises TCO that cloud migration can either reduce or transfer in complex ways. AWS offers two licensing approaches for commercial software.
Licence Included (LI):

Windows Server, SQL Server, RHEL, and SUSE licences can be bundled into the EC2 hourly rate. No upfront purchase, no renewal negotiation — AWS bills per second of use. Ideal when you have no existing licence estate.
Bring Your Own Licence (BYOL):

Existing Microsoft, Oracle, and IBM licences with Software Assurance can be used on AWS Dedicated Hosts or Dedicated Instances. BYOL pricing is lower (you pay for hardware only), but you continue to manage licences yourself.
Managed services eliminate licences entirely:

Amazon Aurora replaces commercial databases like Oracle SE2 for many workloads at a fraction of the licence cost. Migrating from Oracle to Aurora can reduce database software spend by 90 % while improving performance.
AWS License Manager:
Tracks BYOL licences attached to EC2 instances across accounts and enforces limits to prevent accidental over-deployment and compliance violations.

# Create a license configuration to track SQL Server licences
aws license-manager create-license-configuration \
  --name "SQLServerStandard2022" \
  --license-counting-type vCPU \
  --license-count 64 \
  --license-count-hard-limit \
  --description "SQL Server Standard 2022 - 64 vCPU licence pool"

# View licence consumption across accounts
aws license-manager list-usage-for-license-configuration \
  --license-configuration-arn arn:aws:license-manager:us-east-1:123456789012:license-configuration:lic-abc123 \
  --query 'LicenseConfigurationUsageList[*].{Resource:ResourceArn,vCPU:ConsumedLicenses}' \
  --output table
Building a Defensible TCO Business Case

A TCO analysis for executive or finance approval must be defensible — every number should trace to a verifiable source and conservative assumptions should be documented. Overstating savings kills credibility when actuals come in lower.
Start from actual invoices:
Pull real on-premises costs from accounts payable — hardware contracts, colocation bills, maintenance renewals, licence invoices. Do not use industry averages as stand-ins.
Exclude migration costs from the run-rate comparison:
Professional services, training, and migration tooling are one-time costs that distort a steady-state comparison. Present them separately as 'investment to achieve savings.'
Use committed pricing for the cloud column:
On-Demand pricing makes cloud look expensive. A realistic business case applies 1-year Savings Plans or RIs to stable workloads, which is the commitment any rational operator would make.
Include the break-even point:
Show the month when cumulative cloud savings exceed migration costs. A typical enterprise migration breaks even in 14–24 months after accounting for dual-run costs.
Quantify agility benefits separately:

Speed-to-market improvements and the ability to experiment cheaply are real business value but hard to attach a dollar figure to. Present them as qualitative benefits alongside the hard TCO numbers, not rolled into the savings claim.
Key principle:
A TCO model is only as credible as its least defensible assumption. Document every cost input, its source, and the date it was captured. Finance teams will ask.
Benefits of Cloud from a TCO Perspective
Variable cost model eliminates stranded CapEx — capacity that is no longer needed simply stops being billed.
AWS absorbs hardware refresh, facilities management, and physical security — large recurring costs that never appear on cloud invoices.
Managed services (RDS, ECS, Lambda) shift server operating work to application work, improving staff leverage without headcount growth.
Global reach without global data centres — deploy in 30+ AWS regions with no additional facilities investment.
AWS volume pricing benefits all customers — even small teams access the per-unit economics of a hyperscaler.
When On-Premises TCO Can Be Competitive
Fully depreciated hardware with long remaining useful life:
If existing servers are fully paid off and run reliably for another three years, the on-premises marginal cost drops to facilities and staff only.
Very high, sustained compute workloads:
Workloads that run at 80–100 % CPU 24/7 may find dedicated hardware cheaper than On-Demand cloud at that utilisation level — though Savings Plans narrow the gap significantly.
Air-gapped security requirements:
Some government and defence workloads require physical network isolation that no commercial cloud can provide. AWS GovCloud addresses many but not all of these requirements.
Data sovereignty constraints:
Regulations that mandate data never leaves a specific physical facility (not just a country) may require on-premises infrastructure even when cloud is otherwise preferred.
Power Usage Effectiveness and Its TCO Impact

Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) is the ratio of total facility energy to IT equipment energy. A PUE of 2.0 means for every watt consumed by servers, another watt is consumed by cooling, lighting, and UPS systems. Reducing PUE directly reduces the facilities component of on-premises TCO.
Facility Type
Typical PUE
Cost Multiplier on IT Power
Old on-premises data centre
1.8–2.0× the IT power bill
Modern colocation facility
1.4–1.6× the IT power bill
AWS data centres
Included in AWS pricing; not a customer cost

In a TCO model, enter your actual kWh rate and current PUE to compute the true cost of powering on-premises hardware. An enterprise paying $0.10/kWh with a PUE of 1.8 and 100 kW of IT load pays $157,680 per year in energy alone — a cost entirely absent from the AWS bill.
Rightsizing as the Largest TCO Lever

On-premises servers are provisioned for peak-plus-headroom and then run at a fraction of their rated capacity for their entire useful life. Rightsizing — selecting the minimum resource that still meets the P95 demand peak — is the single most impactful TCO optimisation available on both sides of the comparison.
On-premises rightsizing:
Consolidate virtual machines via higher VM density on fewer physical hosts. Deferring a hardware refresh by 12 months by improving virtualisation density can save millions in CapEx.
AWS Compute Optimizer:
Analyses 14 days of CloudWatch CPU, memory, network, and disk metrics. Identifies over-provisioned EC2, EBS, Lambda, and Fargate resources and recommends the smallest right-sized configuration.
Auto Scaling removes fixed sizing:
Instead of provisioning for the peak, configure Auto Scaling to add capacity when CPU exceeds 60 % and remove it below 20 %. The baseline can be sized for average load, not peak.
Serverless removes provisioning entirely:
Lambda and Fargate allocate compute only for the duration of request processing. The 'idle 80 %' problem does not exist for serverless workloads.

# Enable Compute Optimizer for your account (one-time setup)
aws compute-optimizer update-enrollment-status \
  --status Active

# Get Lambda function rightsizing recommendations
aws compute-optimizer get-lambda-function-recommendations \
  --query 'lambdaFunctionRecommendations[*].{
    Function: functionArn,
    Finding: finding,
    CurrentMemoryMB: currentMemorySize,
    OptimalMemoryMB: memorySizeRecommendationOptions[0].memorySize,
    EstimatedMonthlySavings: memorySizeRecommendationOptions[0].estimatedMonthlySavings.value
  }' \
  --output table
Colocation vs Owned Data Centre vs Cloud

The TCO decision is not binary between owned data centre and public cloud. Colocation (housing customer-owned hardware in a third-party facility) is a common intermediate step and a frequent component of hybrid architectures. Each model has a distinct cost profile.
Cost Driver
Owned Data Centre
Colocation
AWS Cloud
Facilities CapEx
Very high
None (OpEx monthly fee)
None
Hardware CapEx
High
High
None
PUE efficiency
Moderate (1.3–1.5)
Elasticity
None
Limited (add racks in weeks)
Instant (seconds/minutes)
Staff to manage
Full team
Reduced (no facilities ops)
Minimal (managed services)
AWS Well-Architected Cost Optimisation Pillar

The AWS Well-Architected Framework's Cost Optimisation pillar defines five design principles for achieving the lowest cost for a given workload on AWS. Applying these principles is the ongoing mechanism for keeping cloud TCO competitive over time.
Implement Cloud Financial Management:
Build the organisational capability (FinOps team, tagging standards, budget reviews) to understand and control spend.
Adopt a consumption model:
Pay only for what you use. Shut down dev and test environments outside business hours; use Auto Scaling instead of fixed fleet sizes.
Measure overall efficiency:
Track business output per dollar spent (e.g. transactions per dollar, API calls per dollar). Rising efficiency means TCO is improving even if absolute spend grows.
Stop spending on undifferentiated heavy lifting:
Use managed services to eliminate patching, backup management, scaling code, and HA configuration from application teams.
Analyse and attribute expenditure:
Tag every resource. Review Cost Explorer weekly. Share per-team cost dashboards so engineering teams feel the cost impact of their architecture decisions.
Common TCO Modelling Mistakes
Even well-intentioned TCO analyses produce misleading conclusions when these common methodological errors are present.
Comparing On-Demand to fully-allocated on-premises:
Always compare On-Demand against on-premises at the same utilisation rate, or compare committed AWS pricing against amortised on-premises. Mixing the comparison frameworks inflates cloud costs.
Excluding the AWS management plane:
AWS Control Tower, AWS Config, CloudTrail, and Security Hub add cost to the cloud TCO. Many first drafts list only compute and storage, understating cloud cost.
Using peak on-premises utilisation for sizing:

If you size the AWS target to match the on-premises peak (rather than average plus headroom with Auto Scaling), you replicate the over-provisioning inefficiency in the cloud and undercut the savings case.
Forgetting egress costs:

Data leaving AWS to on-premises or the internet is billed per GB. Workloads with high data export volumes (analytics pipelines, media delivery, replication) must include egress in the cloud TCO column.
Treating staff savings as headcount reductions:
Finance teams trust models that reallocate staff to higher-value work more than models that assume immediate headcount reduction. The latter requires HR commitments the analysis cannot make.
The FinOps Optimisation Loop

Cloud TCO is not a one-time calculation — it is a continuous optimisation discipline. The FinOps Foundation defines a three-phase loop that organisations cycle through repeatedly to keep cloud spend aligned with business value.
Inform:
Achieve full cost visibility. Activate Cost Allocation Tags, enable CUR, build Cost Explorer dashboards, and share per-team spend reports. You cannot optimise what you cannot see.
Optimise:

Take action on the findings. Right-size over-provisioned resources, purchase Savings Plans for stable baseline, turn off idle environments, and move infrequently accessed data to cheaper storage tiers.
Operate:

Embed cost accountability into engineering culture. Make cost a first-class engineering metric alongside latency and error rate. Include cost impact in architecture reviews and pull-request checklists.
Cloud TCO typically improves 20–30 % in the 12 months after a FinOps programme is established, not because prices fall but because utilisation improves and idle resources are retired.
FinOps insight:

The organisations that spend the most on cloud are often not the ones with the lowest TCO. The ones with the lowest TCO are the ones that have built a culture where every engineer knows the cost of the resources they deploy.
Setting Up Cost and Usage Report for Continuous TCO Tracking

The AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR) is the most granular data source for ongoing TCO tracking. It delivers an hourly or daily CSV/Parquet file to S3 with line-item costs for every resource, usage type, and pricing dimension. Connecting CUR to Amazon Athena enables SQL-based TCO queries across months of history.

# Create a Cost and Usage Report (CUR 2.0)
aws cur put-report-definition \
  --report-definition '{
    "ReportName": "CostAndUsageReport",
    "TimeUnit": "DAILY",
    "Format": "Parquet",
    "Compression": "Parquet",
    "AdditionalSchemaElements": ["RESOURCES"],
    "S3Bucket": "my-cur-bucket",
    "S3Prefix": "cur/",
    "S3Region": "us-east-1",
    "ReportVersioning": "OVERWRITE_REPORT",
    "BillingViewArn": ""
  }'

# Sample Athena query: monthly cost by team tag
-- SELECT
--   year, month,
--   resource_tags_user_team AS team,
--   SUM(line_item_unblended_cost) AS total_cost
-- FROM cur_database.cur_table
-- WHERE line_item_line_item_type = 'Usage'
-- GROUP BY 1, 2, 3
-- ORDER BY 1 DESC, 2 DESC, 4 DESC

CUR data typically lands in S3 within 24 hours. Once the Glue crawler and Athena table are configured, you can run team-level, service-level, and environment-level cost queries without touching the billing console.
Chargeback and Showback: Distributing TCO Accountability

When multiple teams or business units share an AWS account (or organisation), distributing cost responsibility requires either showback (reporting costs to teams for visibility without billing them) or chargeback (actually transferring cloud costs to department budgets). Both mechanisms use the same tagging and CUR infrastructure.
Showback:

Share Cost Explorer filtered views or QuickSight dashboards with engineering teams. Teams see their cost but are not invoiced. Low friction to start — the awareness alone typically reduces waste by 10–15 %.
Chargeback:

Finance integrates CUR data into ERP or financial planning systems. Each department's AWS costs appear in their P&L. Creates strong accountability but requires mature tagging coverage (>90 % of resources tagged).
AWS Billing Conductor:

Creates custom pro-forma invoices for member accounts in an Organisation. Useful for MSPs billing customers, or enterprises that want to show internal business units a marked-up or marked-down view of costs.
Shared resource allocation:

Costs for shared services (NAT Gateways, Direct Connect, shared RDS clusters) can be split evenly, proportionally by usage, or assigned to an overhead cost centre. Document the split methodology before implementation — teams will ask.
Key Takeaways
TCO includes seven on-premises cost categories beyond hardware: networking, storage, facilities, IT staff, software licences, DR, and hidden opportunity costs of over-provisioning.
A rigorous TCO model uses actual invoices and committed AWS pricing (Savings Plans/RIs), not On-Demand prices against on-premises peak-provisioned costs.
AWS Migration Evaluator ingests real utilisation data to right-size the AWS target and produce a defensible business case; the AWS Pricing Calculator models a known architecture.
Migration TCO must include the transition period (dual-run costs), not just the steady-state cloud cost, to set realistic payback-period expectations.
On-premises TCO can compete with cloud for fully-depreciated hardware or near-100 % utilisation workloads — but cloud wins decisively on agility, DR cost, and staff leverage.
